data.history:
The Al Bu Said dynasty was founded by Ahmad bin Said Al Busaidi in 1744, emerging from the small Hinawi Al Bu Said tribe from the interior town of Adam in Oman. Ahmad began as a wood vendor and merchant in Sohar, rising to become its governor in 1737. When Persian forces under Nader Shah invaded Oman, Ahmad held out during a nine-month siege of Sohar, eventually forcing the Persian commander to terms. In 1747, he invited the remaining Persian troops to a banquet at his fort in Barka and massacred them, effectively ending Persian occupation. After defeating rival claimant Bal'arab bin Himyar of the Yaruba in 1749, Ahmad was elected Imam by the Ibadi tribes at Rustaq on 9 June 1749, marking the beginning of the Al Bu Said dynasty.

The dynasty reached its zenith under Sultan Said bin Sultan (1804-1856), known as 'Said the Great,' who built a vast maritime empire extending from Zanzibar to Gwadar. He established Zanzibar as his second capital in 1832 and developed extensive trade networks, including clove cultivation using slave labor. Upon Said's death in 1856, the empire was divided by British arbitration into the Sultanate of Muscat and Oman (under Thuwaini bin Said) and the Sultanate of Zanzibar (under Majid bin Said). The Zanzibar branch ruled until the 1964 revolution, when Sultan Jamshid bin Abdullah was overthrown.

The Omani branch modernized under Sultan Qaboos bin Said (1970-2020), who overthrew his father Said bin Taimur in a British-supported palace coup on 23 July 1970. Qaboos transformed Oman from an isolated, underdeveloped state with only 10 kilometers of paved roads into a modern nation. He ended the Dhofar Rebellion, abolished slavery, established the country's constitution, and developed extensive infrastructure including schools, hospitals, and telecommunications. Today the dynasty continues under Sultan Haitham bin Tariq, who succeeded Qaboos in 2020 after being named in the late Sultan's sealed will.
data.description: The Al Bu Said is the ruling royal dynasty of Oman since 1744, having transformed from a small tribal leadership into rulers of a modern Gulf state. The family currently governs Oman as an absolute monarchy under Sultan Haitham bin Tariq.
data.originStory: The dynasty traces its ancient roots to the Azd tribes who migrated from Yemen after the destruction of the Marib Dam and settled in Dibba (Daba) in Oman, with their progenitor being Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah (Abu Said), a celebrated general under the Umayyad Caliphate. The modern Al Bu Said dynasty was founded by Ahmad bin Said Al Busaidi from the small Hinawi Al Bu Said tribe of Adam, who rose from being a wood vendor to governor of Sohar before liberating Oman from Persian occupation in 1747 and being elected Imam in 1749.
data.modernStatus: Ruling royal family of Oman under Sultan Haitham bin Tariq Al Said since 2020. The Sultan serves as absolute monarch, prime minister, minister of foreign affairs, minister of defence, and chief of staff of the armed forces.
data.nameEtymology: Al Bu Said means 'descendants of Said,' referring to their ancestor Abu Said (Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah). The modern dynasty specifically traces to Ahmad bin Said Al Busaidi.

data.history:
The Al Mualla family is the ruling royal family of Umm Al Quwain, one of the seven emirates of the United Arab Emirates. They have traditionally been at the head of the Al Ali tribe, which originated in Nejd and initially settled on Sinniyah Island before moving to the mainland after water supplies on the island were exhausted. The first known head of the Al Ali when they settled at Umm Al Quwain was Sheikh Rashid bin Majid Al Mualla, who was responsible for the construction of Umm Al Quwain Fort in 1768. The Al Ali tribe was approximately 6,750 strong at the turn of the 19th century, with 1,000 families settled at Umm Al Quwain and others at the inland town of Falaj Al Ali (later known as Falaj Al Mualla), with smaller populations in Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. There was also a Persian group of Al Ali who referred to the Umm Al Quwain section as 'Al Mualla'.

The emirate was formally established when Sheikh Rashid bin Majid Al Mualla built the fort and watchtower in 1768, marking the beginning of organized rule. On 8 January 1820, Sheikh Abdullah bin Rashid signed the General Maritime Treaty with the United Kingdom, accepting British protectorate status along with the other Trucial States. By 1908, Umm Al Quwain was identified as the major boat-building centre on the coast, reflecting its maritime heritage and economic importance in the region.

The family continued to rule through the British protectorate period, with successive rulers maintaining the emirate's independence within the Trucial States framework. On 2 December 1971, Sheikh Ahmad bin Rashid Al Mualla joined the other emirates in forming the United Arab Emirates, becoming one of the founding fathers of the federation. The current ruler, Sheikh Saud bin Rashid Al Mualla, has ruled since 2009 following the death of his predecessor Sheikh Rashid bin Ahmad Al Mualla II, and serves as a member of the UAE Federal Supreme Council. Sheikh Abdullah bin Rashid Al Mualla III has served as Deputy Ruler since 20 April 2004.
data.description: The Al Mualla family is the ruling royal family of Umm Al Quwain emirate in the UAE, traditionally at the head of the Al Ali tribe. The current ruler is Sheikh Saud bin Rashid Al Mualla, who has ruled since 2009 and serves on the UAE Federal Supreme Council.

data.history:
The Al Saud (House of Saud) traces its origins to Mani' ibn Rabiah Al-Muraydi, who settled in Diriyah in 1446-1447 with his clan, the Mrudah. The Mrudah are believed to be descended from the Banu Hanifa branch of the larger Rabi'ah tribal confederation, which played an important role in shaping the Middle East and Arabia from the 6th century. Mani' was invited to Diriyah by a relative named Ibn Dir, who handed him two estates called al-Mulaybeed and Ghusayba. The family name comes from Sheikh Saud ibn Muhammad ibn Muqrin, who died in 1725.

The First Saudi State was founded in 1727 under Muhammad bin Saud, marking the beginning of Saudi territorial expansion combined with Wahhabi religious ideology. At its height, this state included most of modern-day Saudi Arabia, with raids reaching Yemen, Oman, Syria, and Iraq. However, the state was destroyed in 1818 when Ottoman-Egyptian forces captured the Saudi capital of Diriyah and beheaded Abdullah bin Saud in Constantinople. The Second Saudi State (Emirate of Nejd) was established with its capital in Riyadh, but was marked by severe internal conflicts and less territorial control than its predecessor.

The modern Kingdom of Saudi Arabia was established by Abdulaziz bin Abdul Rahman Al Saud (Ibn Saud), who recaptured Riyadh in 1902 at age 20 with a small band of followers. Over three decades, he consolidated control over central Arabia, defeating the Al Rashid clan, conquering the Hijaz in 1925, and formally uniting his realm as the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia in 1932. The discovery of oil in 1938 transformed the kingdom's fortunes, though Ibn Saud maintained traditional Bedouin values while modernizing the state.

Succession within the Al Saud has followed a complex pattern of brother-to-brother inheritance among Ibn Saud's sons. King Saud (1953-1964) was forced to abdicate due to financial mismanagement and power struggles with his brother Crown Prince Faisal. King Faisal ruled until his assassination in 1975 by his nephew Faisal bin Musaid. The kingdom was subsequently ruled by Kings Khalid (1975-1982), Fahd (1982-2005), Abdullah (2005-2015), and currently Salman (2015-present). In 2017, King Salman broke with tradition by appointing his son Mohammed bin Salman as crown prince, making the next succession father-to-son rather than brother-to-brother.
data.description: The Al Saud is the ruling royal family of Saudi Arabia, comprising an estimated 15,000 members with the monarchy holding absolute political power. The family's wealth is estimated at $1.4 trillion, including holdings in Saudi Aramco, making them the wealthiest royal family in the world.
data.originStory: The Al Saud originated from the Banu Hanifa branch of the Rabi'ah tribal confederation. Their ancestor Mani' ibn Rabiah Al-Muraydi settled in Diriyah in 1446-1447 after being invited by his relative Ibn Dir, who granted him two estates. The family takes its name from Sheikh Saud ibn Muhammad ibn Muqrin (died 1725).
data.modernStatus: Ruling family of Saudi Arabia with King Salman as current monarch and Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman as heir apparent. Family wealth estimated at $1.4 trillion including Saudi Aramco holdings, making them the wealthiest royal family globally. Control key government ministries including Defense, Interior, and Foreign Affairs.
data.nameEtymology: The surname 'Al Saud' is formed by adding 'Al' (meaning 'family of' or 'House of') to the personal name of ancestor Saud ibn Muhammad ibn Muqrin, father of the dynasty's 18th century founder Muhammad bin Saud.

migrationPath.description: Al Saud family fled to exile in Kuwait after conquest by Al Rashid rivals, staying nearly a decade before Ibn Saud's return to recapture Riyadh

data.history: The Al Mannai (المناعي) are an Arab tribe that originated from the Banu Tamim confederation, with their presence in the Gulf region dating back centuries. According to historical records, the family's documented involvement in pearl trading began in 1824 when Salem Bin Darwish Al Mannai established a pearl trading business in Bahrain, making them one of the early merchant families in the natural pearl trade that was central to Bahrain's economy until the 20th century. The traditional home of the Al Mannai in Bahrain has been Galali village, where they became established as prominent pearl merchants during the height of the pearl diving industry. In Qatar, the Al Mannai were historically based in the village of Abu Dhalouf, with British historian J.G. Lorimer documenting that 70 families of the Al Mannai tribe inhabited the village in 1908. The British Political Resident noted in 1939 that Emir Abdullah bin Jassim Al Thani's first wife was from the Al Mannai tribe, and that the Qatar section had 70 to 80 fighting men led by Nasser bin Abdullah Al Attiyah. The family brought forth other family names including the Attiyah family (عطية), which gained significant political prominence when the mother of Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, the former Emir of Qatar, was revealed to be from the Attiyah branch of Al Mannai. Today, the Al Mannai have evolved from their pearl trading origins into major business enterprises, most notably through the establishment of Mannai Corporation in Qatar, which has become one of the largest corporations in the country with subsidiary entities extended worldwide and bases in international locations. The tribe has diversified into multiple branches including Al-Binhindi, Al-Badeed, Al-Binejmy, Al-Hashel, Al-Salam, Al-Bughadeer, Al-Ibrahim, Al-Najam, Al-Salah, Al-Hamad, Al-Khamis, Al-Najim, and Al-Rumul, demonstrating their spread and influence across the Gulf region.
data.description: An Arab merchant family from the Banu Tamim tribe, historically known for pearl trading in Bahrain and now operating major business enterprises including Mannai Corporation, one of Qatar's largest corporations with global operations.
data.originStory: The Al Mannai originated from the Banu Tamim tribe and established themselves in the Gulf region, with their documented commercial history beginning in 1824 when Salem Bin Darwish Al Mannai founded the pearl trading business in Bahrain from their traditional base in Galali village.
data.modernStatus: Major business conglomerate through Mannai Corporation in Qatar, one of the largest corporations in the country with subsidiary entities worldwide and international bases. Multiple family branches operate across Gulf states.

data.history: Based on the available research, there is no verified information about the Bani Hafiz family mentioned in the existing data being related to the UAE, Bani Yas confederation, or the Liwa Oasis. The research reveals completely different Bani Hafiz entities that do not support the claimed origin story. The research identifies one Bani Hafiz as a village in Pakistani Kashmir, named after Hafiz Jamal ud din in the late 1700s, whose family became renowned spiritual leaders in the region. The second major reference relates to Hafez Shirazi (1325-1390), the famous Persian poet and Sufi mystic from Shiraz, Iran, who has no apparent connection to Gulf families or tribal confederations. The research also mentions Ahmed Kuftaro (1915-2004), a Kurdish-origin Grand Mufti of Syria, but provides no connection to any Gulf-based Bani Hafiz family. Without verified documentation connecting a Bani Hafiz family to the Liwa Oasis, agricultural expertise, construction ventures, or the Bani Yas confederation in the UAE, the existing origin story cannot be substantiated by the available research.

data.history: The Kalantarian family traces its origins to New Julfa, the Armenian quarter of Isfahan, Iran, established in 1606 when Shah Abbas the Great forcibly relocated over 150,000 Armenians from Old Julfa to serve the Safavid Empire's economic interests. The family name appears prominently in the notable residents of New Julfa, with Petros Kalantarian (1735–1???) documented as a physician in the community. New Julfa became the center of one of the world's greatest early modern trade networks, with Armenian merchant families operating from Amsterdam to Manila, establishing trading posts across the Indian Ocean, Mediterranean, and even reaching the Americas. The Kalantarian family, like other New Julfan Armenians, would have been part of this extensive commercial network that made New Julfa merchants among the most successful international traders of their era. Many New Julfan Armenian families eventually migrated to various parts of the world, including Southeast Asia, Australia, and the Arabian Gulf region, following established trade routes and commercial opportunities. The family's Persian-Armenian heritage reflects the unique position of New Julfa Armenians, who maintained their Armenian Christian identity while operating within the Persian cultural and administrative sphere. The modern diaspora of New Julfan families extends globally, with many maintaining connections to their ancestral community in Isfahan while establishing themselves as successful merchants and professionals in their adopted countries.
data.description: The Kalantarian family is a Persian-Armenian merchant family originating from New Julfa, Isfahan, Iran, part of the historic Armenian trading diaspora that spread across global commercial networks from the 17th century onward. They are documented as having produced physicians and likely participated in the extensive international trade networks centered in New Julfa.
data.originStory: The Kalantarian family originates from New Julfa, the Armenian quarter of Isfahan, Iran, established in 1606 when Shah Abbas the Great relocated Armenians from Old Julfa to serve Safavid commercial interests. The family name Kalantarian likely derives from 'Kalantar,' referring to Persian administrative officials who served as urban administrators and community leaders.
data.nameEtymology: The surname Kalantarian likely derives from 'Kalantar,' a Persian administrative title referring to urban officials who served as tax collectors and community leaders under the Safavid Empire.

data.history: The Al Ghurair family's business history began in the 1930s when Ahmad Al Ghurair and his son Saif were pearl divers in Dubai. During a stormy boat trip to India in the 1930s to sell dates, Saif lost sight in one eye. In 1960, as their business grew successful, Ahmad Al Ghurair founded the Al Ghurair Group to oversee the family's expanding interests. Ahmad later turned management of the family business to his five sons: Saif, Abdulla, Majid, Marwan, and Jomaa. The family pioneered several UAE firsts, building the country's first cement factory, flour mill, and sugar refinery. In 1967, they founded the Bank of Oman (later renamed Mashreq Bank in 1994) in Dubai, which became the largest private bank in the UAE by the 1980s. The group also opened the Gulf's first shopping mall, Al Ghurair Centre, in 1981. In the 1990s, brothers Saif Ahmad and Abdulla Al Ghurair decided to split the business into two complementary companies. Saif retained the Al Ghurair Group (also known as Saif Al Ghurair Group), focusing on manufacturing, real estate, and investments, while Abdulla created Al Ghurair Investment, concentrating on finance, food, and construction. Today, the family controls vast business empires spanning banking, manufacturing, real estate, and retail, with notable figures including Abdulla Al Ghurair (founder of Mashreq Bank, net worth $4.8 billion according to Forbes 2025), and his son Abdul Aziz Al Ghurair who serves as chairman of Mashreq and was former Speaker of the UAE Federal National Council (2007-2011).
data.description: The Al Ghurair family is one of the UAE's most prominent merchant dynasties, controlling two major business conglomerates: the Al Ghurair Group and Al Ghurair Investment. Today they operate diverse enterprises spanning banking (Mashreq Bank), manufacturing, real estate, retail, and construction across multiple countries.
data.originStory: The family's documented business history traces to the 1930s when Ahmad Al Ghurair and his son Saif worked as pearl divers in Dubai, though their earlier tribal or regional origins are not specified in available records.
data.modernStatus: The family operates through two main entities: Al Ghurair Group (chaired by Abdul Rahman Saif Al Ghurair, CEO Majid Saif Al Ghurair) focusing on manufacturing, petrochemicals, and real estate; and Al Ghurair Investment (chaired by Abdul Aziz Al Ghurair) with major stakes in Mashreq Bank, foods, and construction. Abdulla Al Ghurair has a net worth of $4.8 billion (Forbes 2025).

data.history: No verified historical information about the Al Zarooni family is available in the provided research. While existing records claim they originated from the Bani Yas confederation in the Liwa Oasis region and migrated to coastal Abu Dhabi in the early 19th century for pearl trade, none of this information can be confirmed from the research provided. The only documented connection found is that Mahmood Al Zarooni served as a horse trainer for Sheikh Mohammed's Godolphin stables but was disqualified for eight years in April 2013 by the British Horseracing Authority for administering steroids to eleven racehorses. However, this incident does not establish any broader family relationships or provide information about the Al Zarooni family's historical origins, business activities, or tribal connections.

data.history:
The Al Mazrouei are a prominent merchant family from the Bani Yas tribal confederation, originally from the Liwa Oasis region. The research confirms they are considered a subsection of the Bani Yas and formed the majority of the Bedouin component of that federation of tribes, being regarded as the second most influential and likely the most populous section of the confederation. The Liwa Oasis was the homeplace of many of the Mazari (the tribal name for Al Mazrouei), where UK records listed them as the principal property owners among the six Bani Yas tribes in the area, recording 142 families in 1951 and 151 families in 1954, consisting of some 315 houses at the turn of the 20th century. They were closely associated with the Marar tribe at Liwa and were involved in seasonal pearl fisheries, owning boats that were launched from Bandar Rudaim and nearby coastal inlets.

The family's prominence in modern UAE leadership is demonstrated through the marriage of Shamsa bint Suhail Al Mazrouei to Sheikh Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan, the second President of the UAE, on October 18, 1964. The research specifically notes that 'Sheikha Shamsa Bint Suhail was from a famous Al Khamis family of the Al Shikr branch of the Mazaria tribe in Liwa.' This marriage produced eight children, including sons Sultan and Mohammed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan, and daughters who married into other prominent Al Nahyan branches. The Al Mazrouei family's integration into the highest levels of UAE leadership through this union demonstrates their evolution from Bani Yas merchants to influential political allies.

From the 1960s through the 1990s, members of the Mazari, alongside the Suwdan, held numerous senior positions within the Abu Dhabi emirate and at the federal level. Among the most prominent figures was Yousef bin Omar Al-Mazrui, who served as the UAE's federal minister of petroleum until 1994. The Mazari continue to hold prominent positions in Abu Dhabi's political and economic life today, with several individuals occupying senior roles including Yousef bin Omar Al-Mazrui as chairman of ADNOC, and Ahmad bin Mubarak Al-Mazrui as a member of the Abu Dhabi Executive Council and chairman of the Abu Dhabi Health Authority. The family has successfully transitioned from their origins as Bedouin merchants and pearl traders to become key players in the UAE's oil industry and modern governance structure.
data.description: The Al Mazrouei are a prominent merchant family from the Bani Yas tribal confederation who have evolved from pearl traders to modern business leaders, maintaining significant influence in Abu Dhabi's commercial and political circles. The family holds key positions in UAE government and state-owned enterprises, particularly in the energy sector.
data.originStory: The Al Mazrouei originated as the Mazari, a subtribe of the Bani Yas confederation based in the Liwa Oasis. They are traditionally said to originate from the Bani Tamim tribe and settled in the region having already embraced Islam prior to their arrival, though different Mazari communities hold varying views regarding their origins.
data.modernStatus: The Al Mazrouei hold numerous senior positions in UAE government and business, including Yousef bin Omar Al-Mazrui as chairman of ADNOC, Ahmad bin Mubarak Al-Mazrui as member of Abu Dhabi Executive Council, and Suhail Al Mazroui as UAE Minister of Energy and Infrastructure. The family maintains extensive involvement in Abu Dhabi's oil industry and federal government.

data.history: The Al Dabbagh family is a prominent Saudi merchant family with diverse regional branches and varying origins across the Middle East. In Saudi Arabia, the family's modern prominence began with Abdullah Mohammed Ali Al-Dabbagh, who served as Minister of Agriculture and founded the Al-Dabbagh Group (ADG) in 1962. His son Amr Al-Dabbagh, born in 1966, significantly expanded the family's influence by heading the Saudi Arabian General Investment Authority (SAGIA) from 2004 to 2012 and serving as chairman and CEO of the family conglomerate. Amr founded the Jeddah Economic Forum think tank, served on the Jeddah Chamber of Commerce & Industry board, and created international philanthropic initiatives including Philanthropy University in 2015 and the UK-based Stars Foundation (2001-2020). The family faced significant challenges during the 2017 anti-corruption crackdown when Amr was detained at the Ritz Carlton in Riyadh alongside other prominent Saudi businessmen and princes, though he was released without charges in January 2019. Beyond Saudi Arabia, the Al Dabbagh name appears across the Middle East with different origins - in Morocco, Muhammad ibn Abd al-Aziz al-Dabbagh (1928-2008) was a prominent jurisprudent and writer from Fes, while in Iraq, Major-General Khalil Jassim Dabbagh (1916-1969) served as a senior military officer from Mosul whose family had a long tradition in leather-working and trade. The Al-Dabbagh Group continues to operate as a significant family conglomerate in Saudi Arabia, maintaining the family's position as influential merchants and business leaders in the Gulf region.
data.description: The Al Dabbagh family is a prominent Saudi merchant family known for the Al-Dabbagh Group conglomerate and significant roles in government and international business. They have successfully evolved from traditional trading backgrounds into modern business entrepreneurs with interests across various commercial sectors throughout the GCC region.
data.originStory: Origins disputed - The Saudi branch's ancestral origins are uncertain, while other regional Al Dabbagh families have documented different backgrounds. The Iraqi branch from Mosul had a documented tradition in leather-working and trade, with the family name 'Al-Dabbagh' meaning leather smith or leather merchant in Arabic. The Moroccan branch was established in Fes with local prominence in that city.
data.modernStatus: The Al-Dabbagh Group remains a significant family conglomerate in Saudi Arabia. Amr Al-Dabbagh continues as chairman and CEO of ADG, having previously served as governor of SAGIA and founded multiple international philanthropic initiatives including Philanthropy University.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Al-Dabbagh' derives from the Arabic word meaning 'tanner' or 'leather merchant,' reflecting ancestral professions in leather trading and craftsmanship among some family branches.

data.history: The Kanoo family emerged as Persian/Iranian merchants who migrated to Bahrain from southern Iran, establishing one of the Gulf's most prominent trading dynasties. The family's documented history begins with Ahmed bin Mohamed Kanoo, who operated a small foodstuffs trading company in Bahrain. In 1890, his son Yusuf bin Ahmed Kanoo (born 1868 in Bahrain) took over the business at age 22 and transformed it into a major trading empire. Yusuf chartered boats to import commodities directly from Kerala and Mombasa, expanding trade links across the Indian coast and East Africa, making annual trading voyages to India for 4-6 months. He secured crucial shipping agencies including Arab Steamers Ltd (1911), APOC (1913), and later Studebaker and Ford, while also establishing Bahrain's first bank vault (1913) and first petrol pump (1929). His relationship with the British was complex - he served as interpreter for British officials and received honors including MBE (1919) and CIE (1923), yet faced suspicions of being 'in the Arab camp.' The pearl market collapse of the 1930s devastated his finances, forcing him to take heavy loans from the Eastern Bank. Childless himself, Yusuf adopted his brother Mohamed's two sons (Jassim and Ali) and daughter (Mariam) after Mohamed died of plague in 1903-1904. Upon Yusuf's death in 1945, he left his adopted sons just 10,000 Rupees but a collection of lucrative businesses and agencies. The family evolved into the modern Yusuf Bin Ahmed Kanoo Group, established as a conglomerate in 1890, which became one of the largest family-owned multinationals in the Middle East with operations across the GCC, Europe, and Asia, encompassing shipping, logistics, travel, industrial services, and real estate.
data.description: The Kanoo family is one of the most established merchant dynasties in the Arabian Gulf, with origins in Persian/Iranian trading and maritime commerce. They built a diversified business empire that evolved from traditional Gulf trades into modern logistics, shipping, and industrial services across the GCC region through the Yusuf Bin Ahmed Kanoo Group.
data.originStory: The Kanoo family emerged as Persian/Iranian merchants who migrated to Bahrain from southern Iran. The family's founder Ahmed bin Mohamed Kanoo established a small foodstuffs trading company, which was transformed into a major trading empire by his son Yusuf bin Ahmed Kanoo starting in 1890.
data.modernStatus: The family operates the Yusuf Bin Ahmed Kanoo Group, one of the largest family-owned multinationals in the Middle East with over 4,000 employees and operations across the GCC, Europe, and Asia. The conglomerate includes Kanoo Capital, Industrial & Energy, Travel, Shipping & Logistics, and Real Estate divisions, representing major global brands and serving clients like Saudi Aramco, SABIC, and ADNOC.

data.history: The Al Rawas family has a documented historical presence primarily in Baghdad's Bab al-Sheikh district rather than as Omani merchants. The most significant historical evidence comes from Sheikh Muhammad al-Rawas, a Husaynid religious figure who died in 1875 and lived and worked in the Bab al-Sheikh area of Baghdad. Al-Rawas Mosque was founded and built by Sultan Abdul Hamid II in 1893, containing a madrasa and the tomb of Sheikh Muhammad al-Rawas, after whom the mosque was named. This mosque was later demolished by Baghdad Municipality in 1954 to make way for al-Jumhuriya Street, and Sheikh Muhammad al-Rawas's remains were transferred to the cemetery of the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque. The mosque is now recognized as one of the lost mosques of Baghdad. During the Ottoman period, members of the al-Rawi family (which may be related) were among the religious scholars and sheikhs who studied at the Ali al-Bandaniji Takiyya in Baghdad, an important scholarly center in the Bab al-Sheikh area. Additionally, people from the Iraqi town of Rawa are known by the surname al-Rawi and are described as being from noble families of the Prophet's lineage, holding the title of Sharif. However, the research provides no specific information confirming Al Rawas as Omani merchants or their claimed tribal origins in the Azd confederation, suggesting the existing merchant family narrative may be inaccurate or refers to a different Al Rawas lineage entirely.
data.description: The Al Rawas name appears historically connected to religious scholarship and Husaynid lineage in Baghdad, Iraq, rather than as an Omani merchant family as commonly claimed.
data.originStory: Origins disputed - documented evidence shows Al Rawas as a Husaynid religious family in Baghdad, Iraq, with no verified connection to Omani Azdi tribal origins as previously claimed

data.history: The Al Bastaki family is of Persian Achomi origin, tracing their ancestry to the Iranian city of Bastak in southern Fars Province. They are part of the broader Achomi community, Persian-speaking people from Irahistan (including Bastak, Khonj, Evaz, and Jenah) who migrated to the Arabian Peninsula over several centuries. The family follows Sunni Islam, specifically the Shafi'i and Maliki schools of jurisprudence, which distinguished them from the majority Shia populations in southern Iran. The Al Bastaki established themselves as influential merchants and builders in the UAE, playing a crucial role in the development of Dubai's historic Al Bastakiya district, which was named after their hometown of Bastak. Mohammed Al Bastaki supervised the construction of Qasr Al Hosn in Abu Dhabi in 1761, the oldest stone building in the UAE that served as a watchtower and later became the ruling family's palace. The family benefited from the UAE's 1972 Citizenship Law, which granted nationality to Iranians residing in the Trucial States prior to 1925, formally recognizing their status as Ajam Emiratis. Migration from Bastak intensified during the 19th and 20th centuries due to harsh natural conditions and political factors, including Iranian central government imposed import and export taxes. When the Bastakis migrated south to Lengeh, they brought architectural innovations like wind towers with them. Upon settling in Dubai, they constructed coral rock homes adorned with these elegant structures and named their district Bastakiya in tribute to their Iranian homeland. Today, the Al Bastaki family includes notable figures in business, media, entertainment, and medicine, representing the successful integration of Persian merchant families into modern Emirati society while maintaining their cultural heritage through Persian culinary traditions, architectural styles, and family surnames that reflect their Bastaki origins.

data.history: Zahid is an Arabic-origin name and surname that has its roots in the Arabic language and Islamic religious tradition. The name carries multiple meanings based on pronunciation variations, with one primary meaning being 'Altruistic.' In Islamic theological context, Zahid is defined as 'the literal believer in the letter of the Law, opposed to the Sufi, who believes in its spirit,' representing a Zahiri (outsider) perspective versus the inner Batini spiritual approach. The name has gained widespread adoption across Middle Eastern and South Asian regions, particularly in countries with significant Muslim populations. The name appears prominently among notable figures from Pakistan, including cricketers like Zahid Ahmed, Zahid Saeed, and Zahid Mahmood, as well as field hockey players Zahid Pirzada and Zahid Shareef. In the diaspora, Norwegian footballer Ghayas Zahid, born to Pakistani parents from Lalamusa, became historically significant as the first person of Pakistani descent to feature in the UEFA Champions League when he played for APOEL against Real Madrid in 2017. The name has also crossed into other fields, with American wrestler Zahid Valencia achieving prominence as a two-time NCAA Division I national champion and 2025 World Champion. The widespread distribution of the name reflects its cultural significance in Islamic communities and the global diaspora who maintain connections to traditional Islamic values while adapting to modern contexts.

The name appears among notable figures in sports, entertainment, and scholarship across multiple countries.

data.history:
The Al Qahtani is one of the largest and most ancient tribal confederations in Arabia, tracing their lineage to the legendary figure Qahtan, who is considered the progenitor of all southern Arabian tribes. The confederation historically consisted of three main tribal groups: Sanhan, Junb, and Rufaida, with their original homeland centered in Yemen. During various periods of Arabian history, the Qahtan tribe expanded throughout the Peninsula, including a significant migration northward during the Najd period when they settled in the Al-Quwai'iyah region and established considerable influence in southern Najd, becoming one of the most powerful tribes in the area before eventually returning to their southern homeland.

The tribe maintained significant presence and influence during the rise of Islam and continued to play important roles during the establishment of the Saudi states. When Ibn Saud consolidated power in the formation of modern Saudi Arabia, the Al Qahtani confederation, like other major tribes, adapted to the new state system while maintaining their tribal identity. Today, members of the Al Qahtani confederation and its various sub-tribes are distributed across multiple Gulf states including Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates, with various branches like Bani Hajer establishing themselves prominently in countries such as Bahrain.

In contemporary times, the Al Qahtani family has produced notable figures across diverse fields. In sports, footballer Yasser Al-Qahtani gained international recognition. In academia, figures like Nourah al-Qahtani have made contributions to scholarship, while religious scholar Said bin Ali bin Wahf Al-Qahtani has been influential in Islamic studies. However, the family name has also been associated with controversial figures, including Mohammed al-Qahtani, who was detained at Guantanamo Bay for alleged involvement in terrorism, and Saud al-Qahtani, a former Saudi royal court advisor who was dismissed and sanctioned over his role in the assassination of journalist Jamal Khashoggi.

The family's modern presence spans from traditional tribal leadership roles to high-level government positions, including royal court advisors, media figures, and various professional fields. Some branches have maintained connections to traditional territories while others have integrated fully into modern Gulf state systems, reflecting the broader transformation of tribal confederations in the contemporary Arabian Peninsula.
data.description: The Al Qahtani is a prominent Saudi family with members across government service, media, academia, sports, and tribal leadership, including both respected figures and controversial individuals involved in international incidents.
data.originStory: Family members are documented from Saudi Arabia including Kharj and Riyadh regions. The broader Al Qahtani confederation traces descent from the legendary Qahtan, considered the progenitor of southern Arabian tribes, with historical roots in Yemen and subsequent expansion throughout the Arabian Peninsula.
data.modernStatus: Family members hold diverse roles including government positions, academic posts, and media roles, with some branches maintaining tribal leadership while others have integrated into modern state institutions across multiple Gulf countries.

data.history: The Al Ghunaim family appears in documented historical records through two specific but significant connections. The most notable historical reference involves King Fahd of Saudi Arabia's marriage to Seeta bint Ghunaim bin Sunaitan Abu Thnain, though this marriage ultimately ended in divorce. This connection places the Al Ghunaim name within Saudi royal marriage records, suggesting the family held sufficient social standing to form matrimonial alliances with the House of Saud during the late 20th century. In the contemporary business landscape, the family has established a prominent presence in Kuwait's financial sector through Maha Al Ghunaim, who co-founded Global Investment House (GIH). This represents a significant role in Kuwait's modern investment banking and financial services industry. The limited available research provides no information about the family's origins, tribal affiliations, migration patterns, or broader historical development beyond these two documented references. The geographic spread from Saudi royal connections to Kuwaiti business leadership suggests potential Gulf-wide influence, though the specific mechanisms and timeline of this development remain undocumented in the available sources.
data.description: A family name documented in Saudi royal marriage records through King Fahd's marriage to Seeta bint Ghunaim bin Sunaitan Abu Thnain, and represented in contemporary Kuwaiti business leadership through Maha Al Ghunaim, co-founder of Global Investment House.

data.history: The Al Mohannadi (also spelled Al-Muhannadi) is an Arab tribal confederation that emerged as a significant power in Qatar's northern coastal region, particularly in Al Khor (formerly known as Khor Al Shaqiq) and Al Thakhira. According to oral tradition, the tribe was first settled around 1750 by two nomadic Al Muhannadi tribesmen, Mohammed bin Baddah and Majid Al Shuqairi, who discovered fresh water at Ain Hleetan while searching for their missing camels, leading to the establishment of Al Khor as one of Qatar's oldest settlements. The larger portion of the tribal confederation belongs to the Bani Hajer, a Qahtanite tribe, with the leading family being the Al-Misnid family. In the 19th century, the confederation expanded when other tribes including Shahwan and Bani Hajer migrated to the area and intermarried with the original Al Muhannadi, forming new families such as Al-Baddha, Al-Missned, Al-Hassan, and Al Arbeed. Around 1850, many of these tribes united under the Al Muhannadi confederation. The tribe dominated the pearling and fishing industries of northern Qatar, with J.G. Lorimer recording in 1908 that they owned 80 pearl boats manned by 1,200 men, along with 90 other sea-going vessels and 30 fishing boats. The Al Mohannadi maintained significant autonomy from central Qatari authority, with Captain Francis Prideaux noting in 1906 that northern settlements including Al Khor did not pay tribute to Sheikh Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani. In the modern era, the Al Mohannadi have produced notable figures in sports administration, government, and business, including Saoud Al-Mohannadi who served as vice president of Qatar Football Association and FIFA Council member until his death in 2023, and members who founded major companies like Seashore Group.
data.description: An Arab tribal confederation based primarily in Qatar's northern coastal region, especially in Al Khor and Al Thakhira, historically known for dominating the pearling and maritime industries and maintaining significant autonomy from central authority.
data.originStory: According to tradition, two nomadic Al Muhannadi tribesmen, Mohammed bin Baddah and Majid Al Shuqairi, discovered fresh water at Ain Hleetan around 1750 while searching for their missing camels, leading to the settlement of Al Khor by the entire tribe after relocating from Al Khuwayr in northeast Qatar.
data.modernStatus: Members hold prominent positions in government (Ashghal president), sports administration (FIFA Council), and business (Seashore Group founder), particularly concentrated in northern Qatar's Al Khor region.

folkLegends.title: May and Gilan
folkLegends.story: A tale from the Al Mohannadi tribe tells the story of Gilan, a wealthy owner of pearling vessels, who competed with May, a woman with stronger crews. When May's oar-powered boats outpaced his, Gilan drew inspiration from a grasshopper's wings and invented the sail, allowing his vessels to outstrip May's and securing his victory while reaffirming male predominance in pearling.
folkLegends.source: Al Mohannadi tribal oral tradition from Al Khor

data.history:
Abd al-Qays was an ancient Arabian tribe from the Rabi'ah branch of the North Arabian tribes, tracing their lineage to the Adnani genealogical tradition. The tribe originally resided in the area of al-Arid in South Arabia before migrating northwestward to regions extending from Sudayr to al-Kharj. By the 5th century, Abd al-Qays had shifted to nomadism in the southern Najd outside the Tuwaiq escarpment, and in the 6th century migrated northeast to the oases of al-Ahsa, Tarout Island, and Qatif in eastern Arabia, establishing themselves as one of the principal tribes of the historical Bahrain province.

In pre-Islamic times, Abd al-Qays frequently raided Iran, prompting Sasanian king Shapur II (r. 309-379 CE) to lead a retaliatory expedition against them, massacring most of the tribe and relocating survivors to the Iranian province of Kirman. The tribe was predominantly Christian before Islam's advent. During the Islamic conquests, considerable numbers of Abd al-Qays participated extensively in the Arab conquest of Iran, entering Fars and advancing toward Istakhr before settling near Tavvaz in the Iranian coastal mountains and Basra in lower Mesopotamia. In the early 8th century, 4,000 Abd al-Qays warriors joined Qutayba ibn Muslim's campaign into Khorasan.

The tribe's most significant political achievement was establishing the Uyunid Emirate (1076-1238 CE) in al-Hasa after overthrowing Qarmatian rule. Under Abdullah ibn Ali Al Uyuni of the Banu Abd al-Qays, the emirate controlled most of eastern Arabia and Najd for nearly two centuries. The Uyunids allied with Seljuk forces to defeat the Qarmatians at the Battle of Al-Rahlin in 1076, establishing al-Ahsa as their capital. At its height under Muhammad b. Ahmad b. Abu'l-Hussin, the emirate's territory extended from Najd to the Syrian desert, with the Abbasid Caliph al-Nasir granting them authority to protect the pilgrimage route to Mecca.

The majority of modern Baharna are descendants of Abd al-Qays, with minorities from cousin tribes Bakr and Taghlib. Today, most tribal descendants reside throughout the Gulf Cooperation Council countries - Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Qatar, Oman, and the UAE. The Uyunid dynasty is believed to have been the last state in which the population spoke classical Arabic, and their rule saw significant cultural and architectural achievements, including the construction of mosques with Seljuk architectural styles that adhered to Shi'ite principles.

data.history: The Ajman are a Qahtanite Arab tribal confederation descended from Banu Yam, specifically tracing their lineage to Ajman ibn Yasir of the Yam tribe from southwestern Arabia. The confederation formed through alliances of related clans in eastern Arabia during the 17th century, with most members eventually abandoning nomadic life to settle in northeastern Saudi Arabia. The Ajman became major players in eastern Arabian politics during the 18th and early 20th centuries, controlling vast territories and trade routes in the region. Their most famous leader was Rakan bin Hithlain in the 19th-20th century, noted for his poetry and recognized as the maternal grandfather of Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman. In 1861, the Ajman were defeated by Faisal bin Turki of the Second Saudi State after challenging his rule in the 1850s, though Faisal later married into the tribe to strengthen relations. The Ajman supported the Saud Al Kabir branch against Abdulaziz bin Saud during the founding of modern Saudi Arabia. A section led by Dhaydan bin Hithlain joined the Ikhwan movement in 1919, initially providing military support for Ibn Saud before rebelling against him. The Ajman, alongside allies from Utaybah and Mutayr tribes, were decisively defeated by Ibn Saud at the Battle of Sabilla in 1929, ending the Ikhwan rebellion. Today, nearly all Ajman have settled permanently, primarily in the Eastern Province of Saudi Arabia in their traditional territory of Wadi Al-Ajman spanning from Al Nairyah to Judah, with members also holding citizenship across other GCC states including Kuwait, Qatar, and the United Arab Emirates.

data.history:
Al Aunan is documented as part of the Al-Muntafiq tribal confederation, a large Arab tribal confederation that historically controlled southern Iraq and northern Kuwait. According to the research, Al Aunan belongs to the Bani Huchaim division of the Muntafiq confederation, alongside other tribes such as Al Dhuwalim, Al Zayyad, Al Jawabur, Al Towba, Al Aajeeb, Al Abas, and Al Ghazalat. The Muntafiq confederation migrated to Iraq during the Islamic conquests in the 7th century, with most of the tribe tracing its genealogy to Banu 'Uqayl of the ancient Banu 'Amir confederation of Najd, placing them within the Adnanite Arab lineage.

During Ottoman rule, the confederation held control over the Basrah region under Ottoman suzerainty. In 1521, they successfully occupied al-Ahsa and al-Qatif in eastern Saudi Arabia on behalf of the Ottomans before being expelled by Banu Khalid. The confederation is not homogeneous in terms of religion, with centuries of intermarriage and intermingling creating a mix of Sunni and Shia tribes. From the late 18th century onwards, most of the confederation converted to Shia Islam and transitioned from nomadic to sedentary life, taking up agriculture in southern and western Iraq.

Historically, nomadic branches of Al Aunan and related Muntafiq tribes would migrate south into the desert annually during autumn, moving through the Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait and reaching the Arabian Gulf coast, before returning north for summer. The research specifically mentions that these nomadic branches would travel alongside other Muntafiq tribes such as Albu Salih clans, Shuraifat, and Juwarin during these seasonal migrations. During the 1940s and early 1950s, the majority of these nomads settled permanently.

Today, many stateless 'bedoons' in Kuwait belong to the Muntafiq tribal confederation, indicating Al Aunan's continued presence in the region. The overall Muntafiq confederation is divided into three main branches: Bani Malik, al-Ajwad, and Bani Sa'id, with Al Aunan specifically classified under the Bani Huchaim division. The confederation's traditional leaders are Al-Saadun, who are said to be Sharifs originating from Mecca, while maintaining complex genealogical connections that make it 'almost impossible to delineate who is, and who is not part of the Muntafiq.'

data.history:
The Al Bu Shamis is one of three sections of the Na'im tribal confederation, alongside Al Bu Kharaiban and Khawatir, following the Ghafiri tradition. Originally forming the Bedouin component of the Na'im, they retained a predominantly nomadic character into the 1950s with approximately 1,000 individuals, including around 400 armed men. Leadership was held by Muhammad bin Salmin bin Rahmah, who resided at either Sunaynah or Qabil, south of the Buraimi Oasis, exercising authority jointly with his cousins Mani and Hamad bin Ali bin Rahmah in accordance with Bedouin practices.

The Al Bu Shamis emigrated in Eastern Arabia to settle around the Dhahirah and Sunaynah areas, with later migrations leading some to Al Ain and the Buraimi Oasis. Unlike other Na'im sections, they occupied strategically significant oases and coastal settlements, becoming headmen in places like Hamriyah and Al Heera. The tribe is believed to have settled along the coast after being temporarily displaced from Dhahirah in the early 19th century. By 1816-1817, they lost control of Ajman to Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi and his followers from the Al Bu Kharaiban section.

While the Na'im were often involved in disputes with other tribes including the Bani Kaab, Bani Qitab and Al Bu Falah, the Al Bu Shamis remained generally on good terms with other tribes, particularly the Duru and Bani Qitab. Most significantly, although belonging to the Ghafiri faction like the rest of the Na'im, the Al Bu Shamis developed a close alliance with the Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi. This relationship possibly originated from assistance provided by Sheikh Tahnun bin Shakhbut in helping them regain control of Qabil and Sunaynah prior to 1833.

The tribe's most prominent leader, Sheikh Abdulrahman bin Muhammad Al Shamsi of Al Heera, was frequently in conflict with the Na'imi rulers of Ajman, seizing Ajman fort in June 1920 before being removed by British intervention. During the Buraimi Dispute of the 1940s-1950s, the Al Bu Shamis, led by Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad, supported Saudi Arabia and actively fought British forces alongside the Bani Kaab. Following the conflict, some members chose deportation to Saudi Arabia while the majority settled in Al Ain region of Abu Dhabi and the Omani Governorate of Al Buraimi. With the continuing decline of the Na'im tribal federation, the Al Bu Shamis maintained an almost completely separate identity and became closely associated with the ruling Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi, with Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan marrying Alia, daughter of Muhammad bin Salmin bin Rahmah.

Based on the limited available research, Al-Omaro Bani Uqba appears to have been a tribal branch that formed strategic alliances in the Levant region, particularly in the Karak area of present-day Jordan. The most documented aspect of their history relates to their alliance with the Mahamid tribe, which had migrated northward from their original territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz. When the Mahamid reached the Karak region and found themselves outnumbering the existing major tribes, they formed alliances with local clans including Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. This alliance proved significant as it supported the establishment of the Emirate of Mahamid in the region, which was controlled by the Al-Amru clan of the Mahamid confederation. The emirate maintained control for an extended period, though historical sources indicate their rule eventually became increasingly harsh, prompting various clans to unite against them. The specific role of Al-Omaro Bani Uqba within this political structure, their internal tribal organization, and their fate following the emirate's decline remains unclear from available historical records.

The Anizah (Arabic: عنزة, Għanża) is one of the largest and most influential Bedouin confederations in the Arabian Peninsula, tracing their lineage to the Adnani branch of Arab tribes through Wael ibn Qasit. Classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah within the large Rabi'a branch of the Adnanites alongside Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Their existence as an autonomous tribal group predates the rise of Islam in the seventh century, with early historians recording two main branches: nomadic groups in the northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, and sedentary clans in the wadis of Al-Yamama in eastern Najd. The Anizah participated in the Battle of Siffin (657 CE) supporting Ali ibn Abi Talib with 4,000 armored warriors.

During the 10th century, the Anizah settled in the Hijaz region, specifically Khaybar, where they fought and expelled the descendants of Ja'far al-Tayyar from the area. Under Ottoman rule, they became prominent as masters of northwestern Arabian oases, particularly Khaybar and Al-'Ula, collecting tribute known as 'khuwwa' from inhabitants while migrating seasonally between winter settlements and summer pastures in southern Syria. The first Anizah tribe to enter Syria was the Hasana in the mid-18th century, followed by the Wuld Ali, Ruwallah, Fad'an, and Sba'a. The Ruwallah branch became so powerful they developed into a practically independent tribe, engaging in battles with other Anizah branches and becoming arch-enemies of the Shammar tribe.

In Nejd, sparse chronicles relate a process of penetration into northern and western Nejd beginning in the 14th century, where they claimed winter pastures. In the 15th century, the region of Al-Qassim in northern Nejd was rapidly settled through migration, with the majority of this activity by members of Anizah. In the early 18th century, Bedouins of Anizah reached as far as the gates of Riyadh, killing its ruler Zayd Abazara'a in battle. With the rise of the First Saudi State in the late 18th century, Anizah adopted a favorable attitude towards this new power but took little active military part due to their geographical location.

In the 19th century, Sheikh Mashaan Ibn Hathal gained fame leading Anizah clans and unifying them against enemies at the Battle of Al Shimasiyah on June 18, 1825, where he died but the tribe achieved victory. The establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders in the early 20th century severely impacted their nomadic lifestyle, forcing most to settle within new nation-states and adopt Saudi, Kuwaiti, Iraqi, Lebanese, Syrian, or Jordanian citizenship while retaining tribal appellations. Notable ruling families descended from Anizah include the Al-Sabah of Kuwait and Al-Khalifa of Bahrain, both through the Utub Alliance. The relationship of Saudi Arabia's Al-Saud family to Anizah is disputed, with claims of maternal connection while they are officially recognized as part of Banu Hanifa.

data.history:
Bakil (بكيل) is the largest tribal confederation in Yemen, comprising over 10 million people and representing one of the two major surviving ancient tribal confederations alongside Hashid. Both Bakil and Hashid descend from Hamdan (Awsala), tracing their lineage through the Qahtanite branch of Arab genealogy back to Kahlan bin Saba bin Qahtan. The confederation's earliest historical mentions date to the 1st millennium BCE in Sabaean inscriptions, where they were referenced as part of the Kingdom of Sheba's federal structure under the broader Hamdan tribal group.

During the Middle Sabaean period (1st-4th centuries CE), Bakil consisted of three main sha'bs (divisions): Raydah, Amran, and Shibam. In the 3rd century CE, most Hamdan tribes migrated to Syria, but Hashid and Bakil remained in Yemen, switching their allegiance from the declining Sabaean kingdom to the rising Himyarite Kingdom. This strategic alliance allowed them to maintain their territorial control in the northern highlands while other ancient tribal confederations disappeared over time.

Bakil's conversion to Islam occurred in 622 CE when Prophet Muhammad initially sent Khalid ibn Al-Walid to Yemen, though the highland Hamdani tribes showed lukewarm response. Ali ibn Abi Talib proved more successful in converting the Hamdan confederation. After Muhammad's death, Bakil remained steadfast to Islam, refusing to join the ridda (apostasy) movements. The confederation aligned with Ali ibn Abi Talib during the early Islamic civil wars, contributing fighters to Ali's armies at the battles of Siffin and the Camel.

In 893-897 CE, Bakil tribes, along with Hashid, invited Imam al-Hadi Yahya bin al-Hussain to establish Zaidi rule in the northern highlands at Sa'ada. This relationship earned both confederations the designation as 'the two wings of the Zaidi imamate,' though not all tribal members accepted the imams' temporal authority. Throughout subsequent centuries, Bakil successfully resisted various foreign occupations, including helping to expel the Ottomans in 1644. The confederation's territory extends from the northern highlands around Sa'ada to areas near the Saudi border, encompassing parts of modern Sana'a, Marib, Al-Bayda, and Al-Jawf governorates. Today, Bakil maintains decentralized leadership under the Alshaif family, led by Naji bin Abdulaziz Alshaif, and continues to play an active role in contemporary Yemeni politics.

data.history: No direct research information about Bani Hussein is available in the provided sources. The documents focus on unrelated topics including Bani Khalid (a different Arabian tribal confederation), the Siege of El Fasher in Sudan, and general Yemeni tribal structures. While one document mentions tribal clashes within 'the Bani Hussein tribe' near El Fasher, Sudan in March 2023, this appears to reference a local Sudanese group rather than any Arabian Peninsula Bani Hussein. Without specific research evidence about their genealogy, migrations, territorial control, or historical activities, no comprehensive historical narrative can be constructed for this tribal entity.

The Bani Yas is a tribal confederation of Najdi origin that descended, along with the Awamir, from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a, establishing their lineage within the Banu Amir confederation of the Adnani branch of Arab tribes. Around 1700, the House of Al Bu Falah, which held the most power in the confederation, settled with the Bani Yas clans in the Liwa oasis in present-day Abu Dhabi. The tribe later expanded to Al Ain and Buraimi when the Al Bu Falah clan acquired property and land in the Buraimi Oasis. In 1793, the Al Bu Falah migrated to the island of Abu Dhabi due to the discovery of fresh water, and by 1795 they had established the town of Abu Dhabi, which became the seat of the emirate with the construction of Qasr al-Hosn.

According to J.C. Lorimer's Gazetteer (1908-1915), the Bani Yas constituted the largest single tribe among the indigenous tribal population of what is now the UAE, with an estimated population of around 12,000 in the early 20th century, including approximately 2,000 nomadic members. Of the roughly 10,000 settled Bani Yas, about half resided in the Liwa Oasis, 2,800 lived in Abu Dhabi town, around 2,000 in Dubai, and the remainder were distributed among the Buraimi Oasis, offshore islands, and smaller coastal and inland settlements. The confederation consisted of more than 20 sections, with contemporary sources identifying groups such as Al Falasi/Al Bu Falasah (from which the Al Maktoum ruling family descends), Al Falahi/Al Bu Falah (from which the Al Nahyan ruling family descends), Al Qemzi, Al Hemairi, Al Mehairbi, Al Remeithi, Al Mashghouni, Al Hameli, Al Mehairi, Al Suwaidi, Al Qubaisi, and Al Mazrouei.

In 1822, the House of Al Maktoum took over Dubai and established the Emirate of Dubai. The tribe maintained close relations with the Dhawahir tribe but was traditionally at odds with the Na'im and Bani Ka'ab in the Buraimi Oasis. In 1835, members of the Bani Yas settled Khor Al Adaid in southeastern Qatar, expanding their territorial influence beyond the UAE. The confederation's political relationships shifted over time, as associated tribes sometimes sought full integration into the Bani Yas under the authority of the Ruler of Abu Dhabi, while changing regional power dynamics on other occasions encouraged assertions of autonomy.

By the early 1950s, the Bani Yas population had declined to approximately 8,000 due to emigration during the late 1950s when many families moved to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia seeking employment in oil and construction sectors. The 1968 Abu Dhabi census recorded 5,884 members, with significant return migration occurring after Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan's accession and the emirate's oil-driven development. Today, the Al Nahyan (Al Bu Falah branch) leads the tribe and rules Abu Dhabi, while the Al Maktoum (Al Bu Falasah branch) rules Dubai, making them the most influential families in UAE federal government, with the ruler of Abu Dhabi customarily elected as president and the ruler of Dubai as vice president and prime minister.

data.history:
Banu Ghassan was a powerful Arab Christian confederation that emerged as one of the most significant pre-Islamic Arabian political entities from the 3rd to 7th centuries CE. According to Arab genealogical tradition developed during the early Islamic period, they descended from the Azd tribe of South Arabia through their ancestor Jafna, son of Amr Muzayqiya ibn Amir ibn Haritha ibn Imru al-Qays ibn Tha'laba ibn Mazin ibn Azd, though historian Brian Ulrich notes these genealogical links are historically tenuous as these groups appear separately in non-genealogical sources. The tribe's migration northward from South Arabia followed the traditional 'Scattering of Azd' story, with Christian J. Robin's epigraphic studies suggesting they were the main tribal confederation in the Hejaz with a possible base at Medina between the 3rd-4th centuries CE before migrating to Syria.

The Ghassanids established themselves as foederati (allied troops) of the Byzantine Empire in the Levant, serving as a crucial buffer against Persian-allied Arab tribes and Bedouin raids. Their capital was located at Jabiyah in the Golan Heights, and they controlled much of the eastern Levant through tribal alliances extending south to Yathrib (Medina). Under rulers like al-Harith ibn Jabalah (529-569 CE), who received the highest imperial title ever bestowed on a foreign ruler by Emperor Justinian I, the Ghassanids reached their zenith of power, exercising collective authority over all Arab allies of Byzantium. They were staunch supporters of Miaphysite Christianity, which eventually led to conflicts with Orthodox Byzantium and their temporary dissolution by Emperor Maurice (582-585 CE).

The confederation was restored when the Byzantines realized they needed Ghassanid military support against the Persians, and they participated in the final Byzantine-Sasanian War of 602-628. Their political independence ended with the Muslim conquests when the last phylarch, Jabala ibn al-Ayham, led his forces alongside the Byzantine army at the Battle of Yarmouk in 636 CE, where they were defeated by the Rashidun forces. After initially embracing Islam, Jabala ultimately withdrew with his followers to Byzantine Anatolia in 639 CE. Significant remnants remained in Syria, particularly around Damascus and the Ghouta countryside, where many gradually converted to Islam under Umayyad rule and became influential in Islamic administration as governors, military commanders, scholars, and court officials.

During the Umayyad period, the Ghassan played a crucial role in Syrian politics, supporting continued Umayyad rule to secure their interests under the dynasty. They participated in nominating Marwan I as caliph during the tribal summit at their old capital of Jabiyah, and Yazid ibn Abi al-Nims led a revolt in Damascus securing the city for Marwan during the Battle of Marj Rahit. The Ghassan thereafter formed part of the Yaman faction opposing the Qays tribes, with their leader Shabib ibn Abi Malik conspiring to assassinate the pro-Qaysi Caliph al-Walid II. Two prominent Damascene Ghassanid scholarly families achieved particular renown: that of Yahya ibn Yahya al-Ghassani (d. 750s), known as the sayyid ahl Dimashq (leader of the people of Damascus), and Abu Mushir al-Ghassani (d. 833), a prominent hadith scholar. By the 9th century, the geographer al-Ya'qubi recorded the tribe living settled lives in the Ghouta gardens region of Damascus and in Gharandal in Transjordan, marking their transition from a powerful confederation to integrated components of the broader Islamic world.

folkLegends.title: The Scattering of Azd
folkLegends.story: According to the traditional 'Scattering of Azd' story, the Azd tribe migrated northward from South Arabia and different groups split off in different directions, with the Ghassan being one such group that eventually settled within the Roman limes. This migration was traditionally linked to the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in Yemen.

folkLegends.title: Ghassan's Genealogical Origins
folkLegends.story: Arab genealogical tradition traces the Ghassanids as descendants of Jafna, son of Amr Muzayqiya ibn Amir ibn Haritha ibn Imru al-Qays ibn Tha'laba ibn Mazin ibn Azd, linking them through Jafna's brother Tha'laba to the Ansar tribes (Aws and Khazraj) of Medina.

data.nameEtymology: The name appears in ancient sources as variations including Kassanitai (Ptolemy), Casani (Pliny the Elder), Gasandoi (Diodorus Siculus), and Kasandreis (Photios I), suggesting the tribal name predates their northern migration and was recognized in classical geographical sources.

data.history: Banu Al-Sakasek was a branch of the Kinda tribal confederation, which itself formed part of the larger Kahlan confederation originating from ancient Yemen. The Kinda initially dwelt in Bahrain but were expelled to East Yemen, with some groups moving to Nejd where they established a vassal kingdom under Himyarite overlordship. According to the genealogical sources, Banu Al-Sakasek, alongside their tribal relatives Banu Al-Sukun, settled in and ruled parts of Hadramout in eastern Yemen, where they 'fairly ruled' the region according to the tribal records. The tribe's power structure was integrated into the broader Kindite kingdom system, which operated as a client state of the Himyarite empire until the latter's collapse in 525 AD. Following the fall of Himyar, the Kindite territories, including those controlled by Banu Al-Sakasek, came under constant pressure from Bedouin raids originating from Nejd. These sustained attacks eventually led to the destruction of Kindite political authority throughout their former domains. The surviving Kindites, presumably including remnants of Banu Al-Sakasek, were gradually absorbed into the various Najdi tribal federations that emerged in the post-Himyarite period. Some members of related Sakasik/Sakasek groups appear to have migrated northward, with the Sakasic (a Himyarite tribe) settling in northern Egypt around the 3rd century AD, where they established the town that became modern Zaqaziq. Additionally, the Lebanese town of As-Saksakiyah traces its name to the Yemeni tribe of al-Sakāsik, who reportedly settled in southeastern Lebanon in 683 CE before moving to the coastal fortifications and finally establishing themselves in the 8th century.

Banu Yam is a major Qahtani tribal confederation descended from Yam ibn Asbah of the Banu Hamdan group, tracing their lineage to the ancient kingdoms of South Arabia. Originally based in the Najran region of southern Arabia, they became one of the most powerful tribes controlling the Empty Quarter and important trade routes. Unlike many southwestern Arabian tribes, Banu Yam maintained a large bedouin section due to their proximity to the formidable Empty Quarter desert, which allowed them extensive grazing territories and control over trans-desert commerce.

The tribe is notable for being the progenitor of two other major tribal groups: the Al Murrah and the Ajman (Ujman) of eastern Saudi Arabia and the Arabian Gulf coast. Most Yam in Najran follow the Sulaymani Ismaili branch of Shi'ite Islam, with religious leadership currently held by the al-Makrami clan who joined through alliance in the 17th century. Their descendant tribes Al-Ajman and Al Murrah who migrated to the Gulf are predominantly Sunni.

Banu Yam were militarily active throughout their history, conducting repeated raids into neighboring Najd, reaching as far north as Dhruma near Riyadh during the First Saudi State in 1775, causing significant alarm to the Saudi rulers. In 1140, they supported the Sulaymanids in regional conflicts and fought against Banu Nahd in 1154. The tribe also played a role in Aden's governance, with Banu Zurayi from the Yam tribe of Hamdan assuming control of Aden when Al-Mukarram Al-Sulayhi appointed them over it during the Sulayhid period.

Today, members can be found throughout Saudi Arabia due to migration, particularly around Jeddah and Dammam, as well as in Yemen and other Gulf states. Most have transitioned from nomadic life to settled villages, though they maintain their tribal identity and connections. The tribe remains active with populations distributed across the Arabian Peninsula, representing one of the enduring examples of how ancient Qahtani tribal confederations adapted to modern state boundaries while preserving their historical identity.

The Daramikah (Arabic: درامكة, singular Darmaki) are an Arabian tribe and a branch of the widespread Dhawahir (Arabic: ظَوَاهِر) confederation of the United Arab Emirates and Oman. The Dhawahir's early history suggests they originally populated the area of Al Dhahirah in what is today Oman, before settling in the Buraimi Oasis region. By the turn of the 20th century, the Dhawahir tribe numbered 4,500 and consisted of three distinct subsections: the Daramikah, who populated Hili, Qattara, and Mutared; the Jawabir in Al Ain; and the Bani Saad who lived in Jimi.

The Daramikah were semi-nomadic pastoralists who maintained a seasonal migration pattern. They would stay in their villages during the summer date season and move throughout the Trucial States in winter, keeping large flocks of sheep, herding camels, and trading in charcoal which they burned from the fertile woodlands of the oasis. The oasis was irrigated by a highly effective falaj system that supported this wood-burning industry. The Daramikah owned much of the cultivated land and property in the Al Ain oasis, with the exception of Buraimi village itself.

The tribe's relationship with the Al Nahyan rulers of Abu Dhabi became increasingly strained in the latter 19th century. Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan, known as 'Zayed the Great', systematically increased his hold on Al Ain by buying date groves and water rights, predominantly from the Dhawahir including the Daramikah. When this policy began changing the pattern of ownership throughout the oasis, the Dhawahir rebelled in 1877. After a month-long conflict, Zayed prevailed and took two Dhawahir sheikhs hostage to guarantee the tribe's good behavior. In 1891, he marched on the oasis again with support from Dubai, commanding 30 horsemen and 300 camel riders, and took the Dhawahir's main settlement 'Ain Dhawahir (now simply Al Ain).

Following their defeat, the Al bu Falah of Abu Dhabi bought out much of the property previously owned by the Dhawahir, including the Daramikah, though they continued to cultivate the land in various oases including Al Ain, Jimi, Hilli, Qattarah and Mu'tirid. The Daramikah have given their name to a small watchtower located in the Qattara Oasis in Al Ain, which protected the falaj irrigation system. Archaeological finds at Qattara include Wadi Suq era chlorite jugs and bowls, late Bronze Age short swords and daggers, and a notable Bronze Age pendant made from electrum depicting entwined horned animals. Today, the Daramikah maintain their identity as part of the larger Dhawahir confederation, which has long had a strong alliance with the ruling family of Abu Dhabi, the Al Nahyan, and the Bani Yas confederation.

The Duru (Arabic: الدرو، singular Al Darai الدروع) is a relatively small but historically significant Bedouin tribe of the United Arab Emirates. Despite their limited numbers, the Duru managed to involve themselves in numerous territorial conflicts throughout the Trucial States during the 20th century, particularly around the strategically important Buraimi and Liwa oases. The tribe maintained their traditional dar (territory) in these areas while engaging in complex alliances and rivalries with neighboring tribes. The Duru belonged to the Ghafiri faction and maintained persistent conflicts with other tribes, most notably the warlike Awamir who encroached upon their territory, and the Rashid tribe, who were described as maintaining 'lively tribal enmities' with the Duru.

In 1877, the Duru joined forces with the Awamir tribe to conduct raids against Dubai, while northern Bani Qitab simultaneously raided Abu Dhabi, before peace was eventually brokered. Although the Duru considered themselves dependents of Dubai and maintained good relations with the Al Bu Shamis, territorial pressures intensified in the early 20th century. During the 1920s, major fighting erupted between Bedouin tribes in the Liwa and Buraimi regions, with the Duru forming part of a southern confederation alongside the Awamir and Al Bu Shamis against the northern alliance of Manasir and Bani Yas of Dhafra.

The conflicts escalated significantly under Sheikh Sultan bin Zayed Al Nahyan's rule of Abu Dhabi. In December 1923, Sultan marched against the Duru, prompting them to raise a force with the Awamir and Bani Qitab of Falayah that successfully defeated a party of 20 riders from Abu Dhabi, killing seven and capturing the rest. The escalating conflict saw both sides conducting retaliatory raids, with the Duru targeting Dubai in revenge for its support of Sultan's forces. Seeking external protection, the Duru, along with the Al Bu Shamis, turned to Ibn Jaluwi who was eager to extend Saudi influence among the Trucial States tribes.

This decision to seek Saudi protection contributed significantly to the territorial arguments that would later form part of the Buraimi Dispute between Saudi Arabia and the Trucial States. As portions of various tribes, including elements influenced by Duru conflicts, became Saudi citizens while others remained under British protection, the tribe's political choices had lasting geopolitical consequences that extended well beyond their limited numbers would suggest.

The Ghatafan (غطفان) were a major pre-Islamic Adnanite tribal confederation descended from Ghaṭafān ibn Saʾd ibn Qays ʿAylān, making them part of the larger Qays tribe. They inhabited the Wadi al-Rumma area of Najd between the Hejaz mountains and Jabal Shammar, with territory extending northeast of Medina. The confederation was divided into several main branches: the Banu Abs (descendants of ʿAbs ibn Baghīd ibn Rayth ibn Ghaṭafān) in the easternmost territory, the Banu Dhubyan (from Dhubyān ibn Baghīd ibn Rayth ibn Ghaṭafān) which included the major subtribes Banu Murra, Banu Fazara, and Tha'laba, the Banu Ashja in the westernmost area, and the Anmar who inhabited the Qassim region of Najd. The Ghatafan were recognized as one of the 'Great Skulls of Arabia' - the most powerful and influential pre-Islamic tribal confederations, with descendants later found throughout Arabia and the Maghreb.

During the early Islamic period, Ghatafan played a significant role in conflicts with Muhammad's expanding Muslim community. They participated in several military engagements against the Muslims, including the Invasion of Dhi Amr in September 624 CE, the Expedition of Dhat al-Riqa, and the Invasion of Dumatul Jandal in July 626. Most notably, they allied with the Quraysh and other confederate tribes in the Battle of the Trench (627 CE), where they formed part of a coalition army estimated at 7,500-10,000 men. The Banu Nadir had successfully enlisted Ghatafan support by paying them half of their harvest from Khaybar, securing approximately 2,000 men and 300 horsemen led by Unaina bin Hasan Fazari.

The Ghatafan's most controversial involvement came during the Battle of Khaybar in 628 CE, where they had been contracted by the Banu Nadir to provide 2,000 men and 300 horsemen to defend the Jewish settlement against Muslim forces. However, during the actual battle, the Ghatafan forces failed to provide the promised reinforcements. Various accounts suggest they were either bought off by Muslim negotiators or turned back due to rumors that Muslims were attacking their own strongholds. This failure to honor their commitment to Khaybar marked a significant moment in their relations with both Jewish tribes and the emerging Muslim state.

In the 6th century, the legendary chieftain Zuhayr ibn Janab of the Banu Kalb tribe destroyed a Ghatafan sanctuary called 'Buss' that had been constructed to rival the Ka'aba in Mecca. This event demonstrates the significant religious and political influence Ghatafan wielded in pre-Islamic Arabia, as their sanctuary was considered important enough to challenge Meccan religious supremacy. According to different accounts, Zuhayr's destruction of the sanctuary was motivated either by tribal rivalry - viewing the Ghatafani sanctuary as an assertion of superiority over his own Kalb tribe - or as a favor to the Quraysh of Mecca to eliminate religious competition.

folkLegends.title: The Sanctuary of Buss
folkLegends.story: The Ghatafan tribe constructed a religious sanctuary called 'Buss' to rival the Ka'aba in Mecca. The legendary chieftain Zuhayr ibn Janab of the Banu Kalb became enraged when he learned of this, exclaiming 'By God, that will never happen while I am alive! I will never leave Ghatafan alone while they take a haram.' He gathered his Kalbi tribesmen, raided and defeated the Ghatafan in their dwelling place, and destroyed their sanctuary. Afterward, 'he acted graciously toward Ghatafan', releasing their women and cattle.
folkLegends.source: Arab historical traditions recorded by Abu al-Faraj al-Isfahani

data.history: The Hamudiyin are a distinct section of the Sharqiyin tribe of the United Arab Emirates, historically concentrated along the eastern coast in areas including Wadi Sharm and Wadi Zikt in what is now Fujairah Emirate. As part of the broader Sharqiyin confederation, they were traditionally aligned with the Ghafiri tribal faction and were among the dominant groups along the Trucial States' eastern coastline. The Sharqiyin, including the Hamudiyin section, were originally dependencies of Sharjah but engaged in numerous rebellions throughout the 19th and early 20th centuries seeking independence. Under leaders like Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah Al Sharqi of the Hafaitat section, they successfully resisted Sharjah's control, culminating in practical independence from 1901 and eventual British recognition of Fujairah as a separate Trucial State in 1952. The Hamudiyin subsisted primarily through agriculture, pearling, and fishing, living in barasti palm frond houses as late as the 1960s. Historical records from 1908 describe Hamudiyin settlements like Sharm with 40 houses and Dadna with 50 houses, complete with detailed accounts of their livestock and date palm cultivation along the wadis. The area of Wadi Zikt was populated mainly by the Sharqiyin tribe, corresponding to the tribal section of Hamudiyin, while the Wadi Sharm area was also mainly populated by the Sharqiyin tribe, specifically the Hamudiyin section. By 1968, a census showed 90% of the tribal population of Fujairah was Sharqiyin, making them the dominant tribal group in the emirate.
