data.history:
The Huwayla tribe is documented as a sub-tribe of the Qawasim (Al Qasimi) clan, with their most significant historical role being the establishment of their rule in Sharjah around 1727. According to historical records, the Huwayla tribe of the Qawasim clan established itself in Sharjah circa 1727, declaring the emirate independent and marking a crucial political development in the Trucial Coast region. This establishment transformed Sharjah into one of the wealthiest towns in the region, building upon a settlement that had existed for over 5000 years prior to Huwayla control.

The Huwayla's governance was formalized through the broader Al Qasimi dynasty structure, which has ruled Sharjah since the 18th century. Their political authority was internationally recognized when Sheikh Sultan I signed the General Maritime Treaty with Britain on 8 January 1820, accepting protectorate status to prevent Ottoman Turkish expansion into the region. Like neighboring emirates including Ajman, Dubai, Ras Al Khaimah, and Umm Al Quwain, Sharjah under Huwayla-Qasimi rule was recognized as a salute state by the British, albeit of the lowest class with 3-gun honors, reflecting its strategic importance on the maritime route to India.

The Huwayla tribe's rule continued through the Al Qasimi dynastic line into the 19th and 20th centuries, maintaining control over Sharjah's political and economic affairs. Their governance extended inland to areas like Wasit Suburb by the early 20th century, though the territory between the city and Al Dhaid remained under the control of interior tribes. The Huwayla-Qasimi leadership successfully navigated the transition from British protectorate status to full independence, with Sharjah joining the United Arab Emirates on 2 December 1971 as a founding member.

data.history: Al-Kabanah (الكبانة) appears in Sudanese genealogical records as one of the riverine clans (Manasir al-Nil) of the Manasir people along the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in Northern Sudan. According to Sudanese tribal documentation, al-Kabanah is listed among the settled Manasir clans who pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils near the Nile, alongside other riverine groups like al-Wahabab, al-Suleimaniyah, and al-Diqeisab. The Manasir trace their origins to various ancestral lines including Mansur bin Qahtan of the Qahtani Arabs, descendants of al-Abbas (uncle of Prophet Muhammad), or connections to the Kawahla and Shaiqi clans. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community, including al-Kabanah, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and other tribal minorities who adopted a common genealogical identity for mutual rights and territorial claims. The connection between this Sudanese al-Kabanah clan and any Arabian Peninsula tribal group of the same name remains undocumented in available sources. The Sudanese al-Kabanah clan is classified among the riverain Manasir (Manasir al-Nil), distinguishing them from the nomadic Bedouin Manasir (Manasir al-Badiyah) who inhabit the Bayuda Desert. As riverine people, al-Kabanah members engage in small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils in the immediate vicinity of the Nile, with dates being their most important cash crop sold on national markets.

data.history:
Khuza'a (Banu Khuzā'ah) is an ancient Azdite, Qahtanite tribe that emerged as one of the main ancestral tribes of Arabia. Most traditionalists trace their origins to Amr ibn Luhay (also known as Amr ibn Rabiah), who belonged to the Azd confederation that migrated north from Yemen following the breach of the Marib dam around the 5th century CE. The tribe's name derives from the Arabic root meaning 'to separate' (khuza'a), as they broke away from other Azd tribes during this great migration and settled around Mecca in the Hijaz region. However, there is scholarly debate about their lineage, with some genealogists like Ibn Iṣḥaq arguing they descended from Adnan rather than Qahtān, supported by prophetic traditions (hadith) that trace their ancestry to Amr ibn Luhay bin Qum'ah bin Ilyās bin Muḑar bin Nazār bin Ma'd bin 'Adnān.

For approximately 500 years before Islam, Khuza'a ruled Mecca as custodians of the Kaaba, having expelled the previous Jurhum tribe guardians. Amr ibn Luhay, their first ruler of the Sacred House, is credited with introducing idol worship to Mecca, including the famous idol Hubal brought from Syria-Palestine. The tribe successfully defended their custodianship against various challengers, including the Banu Qays Aylan and Hawāzin tribes. Their control ended in the 5th century CE when Qusayy ibn Kilab of Quraysh married into the Khuza'i ruling family (Hubay bint Halil bin Habshah) and eventually seized control through the famous 'Day of Abṭaḥ' battle, though some accounts suggest he purchased the custodianship from a drunk Khuza'i leader named Abū Ghabshān for wine and a camel.

During Muhammad's time, Khuza'a formed a crucial alliance with the Prophet, participating in the Treaty of Hudaybiya in 628 CE. When their Bakr ibn Abd Manat enemies attacked them at al-Watīr in 630 CE with Qurayshi support, this breach of the treaty provided the justification for Muhammad's conquest of Mecca. Khuza'a warriors had warned Muhammad of the Confederate armies during the Battle of the Trench, demonstrating their continued loyalty. The tribe played significant roles in early Islamic history and later in the Abbasid rise to power, with members holding senior positions including Vizier to the Caliph, Master of Post, holders of the Royal Seal, Governors of provinces, and Head of Abbasid Armies.

The Khaza'il branch established the Kingdom of Khaza'il (1534-1921) in Mesopotamia, controlling the Middle and Lower Euphrates region from Anah to Basra and resisting Ottoman rule for centuries. At their height in the 17th-19th centuries, they exercised military, economic, and political sovereignty, creating Silk Road taxation mechanisms and maintaining agricultural monopolies. They were instrumental in establishing Shiite dominance in Mesopotamia and ending Ottoman rule in Iraq. Today, Khuza'a descendants live primarily in Saudi Arabia around Mecca, with significant populations organized into various recognized family branches in Iraq, Jordan, Palestine, and other Middle Eastern countries.

folkLegends.title: The Great Separation from Azd
folkLegends.story: When the Azd tribes left Yemen after the breach of the Marib dam, Khuza'a separated from their kinsmen during the migration. According to Arab linguists, they 'lagged behind their companions' (khaza'a 'an aṣḥābihi) when the tribes dismounted at Baṭn Murr, staying behind at the camp at Karākir while the others continued. This separation gave them their name, meaning 'to separate' or 'to break away.'

folkLegends.title: Amr ibn Luhay's Journey to Syria
folkLegends.story: Amr ibn Luhay traveled to Syria-Palestine to seek a cure for a disease he was suffering from. There he found the people worshiping idols and liked this religion, so he brought back the idol Hubal to Mecca and called on the people to worship it. Hubal had the figure of an old man with a long beard made of carnelian, with its right hand cut off but later replaced with a golden hand by the Quraysh.

folkLegends.title: The Damaging Transaction of Abū Ghabshān
folkLegends.story: When Ḥalīl became old, he passed control of the Sacred House to Abū Ghabshān Salīm bin Amr al-Khuza'i. One day while drinking with Quṣay, Abū Ghabshān became drunk and sold the control of the Sacred House to Quṣay for a skinful of wine and a young camel. This led to the saying 'More damaging than the transaction of Abū Ghabshān.'

data.nameEtymology: The name Khuza'a derives from the Arabic root meaning 'to separate' (khuza'a), as they broke away from other Azd tribes during the great migration from Yemen. Arab linguists suggest it came from the phrase 'khaza'a 'an aṣḥābihi' meaning 'he has lagged behind his companions,' referring to how they stayed behind their people when coming from Marib.

data.history:
The Lakhmids were an Arab dynasty of the Banu Lakhm tribe that ruled a vassal kingdom of the Sasanian Empire from the late 3rd to early 7th centuries CE, with their capital at al-Hira in southern Iraq. Founded by Amr ibn Adi ibn Nasr around 293-302 CE, who is identified in Pahlavi and Coptic inscriptions as 'Amr ibn Lakhm,' the kingdom served as a crucial buffer state protecting Persia's western frontier from nomadic Arab incursions. The Banu Lakhm belonged to the Qahtanite lineage, tracing their ancestry to the southern Arabian tribes that had migrated northward in pre-Islamic times. After an early period where Amr's successor Imru al-Qays I defected to Rome and converted to Christianity, little is heard of the Lakhmids until the 5th century when they re-emerged as powerful commanders in Sasanian campaigns against the Byzantines and rulers over the Arab tribes of northern Arabia.

The dynasty reached its apex under al-Mundhir III (r. 503-554), who defeated the Byzantine-allied Ghassanids at the Battle of Callinicum in 531, forced the Romans to pay tribute, and helped extend Sasanian influence across southern and western Arabia during his fifty-year reign. The Lakhmids were marked by their role as intermediaries between the Persian Empire and Arabian Peninsula tribes, controlling key trade routes and wielding significant influence over Arabian tribal affairs. The kingdom's decline began after al-Mundhir III's death, culminating when the last Lakhmid king, al-Nu'man III (r. 580-602), converted to Christianity and was subsequently deposed and executed by Sasanian emperor Khosrow II in 602.

Branches of the Lakhm tribe had also established themselves in Byzantine Syria from at least the 4th century, as evidenced by the Namara inscription dated to 328 CE. These Syrian Lakhm dwelt in the southern parts of the region near the tribes of Judham and Amila, forming close alliances and fictitious genealogical links that made them 'brother' tribes. During the Muslim conquest of Syria in the 630s, Lakhm tribesmen fought on both Byzantine and Muslim sides due to pre-existing tribal divisions and rivalries. The tribe's unclear allegiance during the conquest is reflected in Caliph Umar's order to exclude them from shares in war spoils around 638, though they were eventually incorporated into the Muslim polity.

Throughout the early Islamic period, the Lakhm of Syria and Palestine were almost invariably grouped with the Judham tribe, fighting together under the same banner in major conflicts including the Battle of Siffin in 657 and later Umayyad military campaigns. Along with the Judham and other tribes like Kinana and Azd Sarat, the Lakhm formed part of the Arab tribal soldiery of Jund Filastin (military district of Palestine) during the Umayyad period. Despite their integration with other tribes, their nisba (epithet) continued to carry prestige due to 'its archaic flavour, the glorious memories which it recalled' of the kings of al-Hira, with notable figures as late as the 9th and 10th centuries continuing to claim descent from the tribe.

data.history:
The Manasir al-Badiyah (Bedouin Manasir) are the nomadic branch of the larger Manasir tribal confederation of Northern Sudan, distinct from their riverine counterparts along the Nile. According to oral traditions recorded in tribal genealogies, the Manasir trace their origins to multiple possible ancestors within the Qahtani lineage. Some claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arab Qahtaniin lineage, while others trace lineage to Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, or to descendants of a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab who migrated after internal conflicts. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities who established common genealogical recognition for mutual rights and territorial claims.

The Bedouin branch (Manasir al-Badiyah) is organized into four main clans: al-Khabra, al-Hamamir, al-Muleikab, and al-Kagbab, who traditionally shared the grazing grounds of Abisba' and Sani. These nomadic clans maintained a pastoralist lifestyle centered on herding goats, sheep and camels in the Bayuda Desert adjacent to the Nile. Their nomadic life was typically limited to the rainy season, coinciding with the annual inundation of the Nile, when they would move their livestock to desert valleys for grazing.

During dry seasons, the Manasir al-Badiyah clans engaged in seasonal migrations to various territories according to traditional grazing agreements. Some clans migrated west to the Kababish territory (Khala' Kabushiyah), others moved to the grazing grounds of Wad Hamid in Ga'ali country, or to Rubatab territories. This system of seasonal movement allowed them to maintain their herds while respecting territorial boundaries of neighboring tribes. Early travelers witnessed Beja and Bisharin influences from the Red Sea Hills among the Manasir, and similarities with Nubian burial customs can still be observed, reflecting the complex cultural amalgamation that formed the tribe.

Like other Sudanese tribes, the Manasir do not have unique tribal marks (Shilukh), but copy the scarification patterns of either their upstream or downstream neighboring tribes. The current division between Riverain Manasir (Manasir al-Nil) and Bedouin Manasir (Manasir al-Badiyah) reflects their adaptation to different ecological niches along the Nile and in the adjacent desert regions, with the Bedouin branch maintaining longer adherence to traditional nomadic practices.

data.history:
The Mazrui are an Arab tribal confederation with documented presence across multiple regions of the Arabian Peninsula and East Africa. The research reveals distinct but related Mazrui lineages: the East African branch that established political control over parts of Kenya from the 18th-20th centuries, and the UAE branch that forms a major subsection of the Bani Yas confederation. The East African Mazrui, originating from Oman, governed Mombasa and other coastal areas throughout the 18th century, maintaining opposition to the Omani Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty that ruled Zanzibar. They demonstrated their resistance by allying with Portuguese forces on at least one occasion to attack Stone Town, showing their willingness to align with European powers against rival Arab dynasties.

The UAE Mazari branch represents a separate but potentially related lineage within the broader Mazrui tribal network. According to the Amir of the Mazari at Adhen and Ghayl, this branch traces its origins to the Bani Tamim tribe and had already embraced Islam prior to settling in the UAE region at an unspecified period. Originally belonging to the Ghafiri faction and following the Hanbali school of Islamic jurisprudence, the Abu Dhabi Mazari generally came to adhere to the Maliki school. The Liwa Oasis served as their primary homeland, where they owned significant date gardens and practiced seasonal pastoralism, moving with their camel herds to grazing areas in Dhafra or Khatam during winter months.

Conflicts in the 19th and early 20th centuries significantly impacted Mazari tribal unity and territorial control. An outbreak of hostilities between Bedouin tribes in the Dhafra region created divisions within the Mazari, with some groups seeking Saudi protection while others migrated to Abu Dhabi and its islands. After Sheikh Hamdan bin Zayed Al Nahyan arranged a truce, the Mazari returned to Dhafra, but continued fighting allowed Abdelaziz Ibn Saud of Saudi Arabia to increase his influence over the tribes and exact zakat taxation from them. These shifting allegiances and internal schisms later formed part of the Saudi claims that led to the Buraimi Dispute.

In the modern UAE, the Mazari have achieved significant political and economic prominence within Abu Dhabi's government structure. From the 1960s through the 1990s, members of the Mazari, alongside the Suwdan, held numerous senior positions within the Abu Dhabi emirate and at the federal level. The ruler's wife is a member of the family, and several individuals currently occupy senior roles, including positions as chairman of ADNOC, members of the Abu Dhabi Executive Council, and directors of various authorities and development councils. The East African Mazrui similarly played important roles in resistance movements, actively opposing British colonial rule alongside the Kikuyu and Kamba peoples when the British East Africa Protectorate was established in the late 19th century.

data.history:
Al-'Ulwa is one of the three major branches of the Mutayr tribal confederation, alongside Banu Abdullah and Braih. According to genealogical sources including Ahmad al-Qalqashandi (d. 1418), the Mutayr tribe belongs to Ghatafan who are descendants of Ishmael son of Abraham. DNA studies have confirmed that Mutayr members share a common ancestor and predominantly belong to the J1 haplogroup network. Al-'Ulwa is internally divided into seven main clans: Al-Muhahah, Al-Jiblan, Al-'Awanah, Al-Rukhman, Al-Mala'bah, Al-Sahabah, and Al-Bara'sah.

Mutayr's original homelands were the highlands of northern Hejaz near Medina. At some point in the 17th century, the tribe began a large-scale migration eastwards into central Arabia, displacing many other Bedouin tribes in the area, such as Harb and Anizzah who were forced to move northwards. This migration brought them into rivalry with Utaybah, who inhabited roughly the same areas as Mutayr in central Arabia. Because Mutayr were the dominant nomadic tribe of Al-Qaseem, which was the main bone of contention between the clans of Al Saud and Al Rashid vying for control of central Arabia in the early 20th century, they came to play an important role in the history of Arabia during that era.

The tribe was led by Faisal Al-Dewish, who frequently changed sides in the conflict between the two Saudi Arabian leaders. In 1912, Abdul Aziz Ibn Saud undertook to settle the nomads in newly created villages (hijras), where the bedouins were indoctrinated into a puritanical form of Islam and became warriors for Ibn Saud's cause as the Ikhwan. Faisal Al-Dewish, who was from the Al-'Ulwa branch, led the Ikhwan movement enthusiastically, providing Ibn Saud with crucial military support. The most important Mutayri settlement was al-Artawiyya, at the northern edge of the Dahna desert.

In 1920 Al-Dewish led an attack by the Ikhwan on Kuwait at al-Jahra, and later joined other sections of the Ikhwan in the conquest of the Hejaz on behalf of Ibn Saud in 1924. However, Al-Dewish and other Ikhwan leaders subsequently led a rebellion against Ibn Saud, seeking to take over the newly conquered provinces for themselves and claiming that Ibn Saud had abandoned the true faith. Ibn Saud defeated the rebels at the Battle of Sabilla in northeastern Nejd, and Al-Dewish sought refuge with the British in Iraq before being handed over to Ibn Saud. He was imprisoned and died not long afterwards. Today, nearly all members of the tribe are settled in the cities and towns of Saudi Arabia, making up to 400 villages across the country, with large populations also in Kuwait.

data.history:
The Qahtanites (Arabic: قَحْطَانِيون), also known as Banu Qahtan or al-Arab al-Ariba (the Pure Arabs), are the southern Arabian tribes originating from modern-day Hadhramaut and Yemen. The term 'Qahtan' is mentioned in multiple Ancient South Arabian inscriptions found in Yemen, and some Arab traditions believe that the Qahtanites are the original Arabs. According to Arab genealogical tradition, they descend from a semi-legendary ancestral figure named Qahtan and his 24 sons, who are considered the progenitors of Yemen and controllers of the Arabian Peninsula. The genealogists disagree about Qahtan's exact pedigree - some trace him back to Ishmael through a lineage including al-Hamaysa, Tayman, and Nabt, while others equate him with the biblical Yoqtan (Joktan) son of Eber, and still others connect him to Jokshan, son of Abraham and Keturah.

The Qahtanite people are traditionally divided into two major sub-groups: Himyar (the settled Arabs of the south) and Kahlan (their nomadic kinsmen). Among Qahtan's sons are noteworthy figures like A'zaal (believed to have been the original name of Sanaa), Hadhramaut, Jurhum, and Ya'rub, whose son Yashjub is the father of Saba. All Yemenite tribes trace their ancestry back to Saba through either of his two sons, Himyar or Kahlan. The Kahlan division consists of four main subgroups: the Ta or Tayy, the Azd group which invaded Oman, the Amila-Judham group of Palestine, and the Hamdan-Madhhij group who mostly remain in Yemen. Major Kahlan tribes include Azd (encompassing Aus, Khazraj, Bariq, Ghassan, Khuza'a and Daws), Hamdan, Khath'am, Bajila, Madhhij, Murad, Zubaid, Ash'ar, Lakhm, Tayy (Shammar), and Kinda.

Pre-Islamic migrations saw various Qahtanite groups moving northward from their Yemen homeland. The Ghassanids (ca. 250 CE) represented the last major non-Islamic Semitic migration northward out of Yemen, initially settling in the Hauran region and eventually spreading to Palestine and Jordan, briefly securing governorship of Syria away from the Nabataeans. Following the rise of Islam in the 7th century, the Qahtanites became heavily involved in the Arab conquests, migrating to newly conquered territories and intermingling with local populations across the Levant, Mesopotamia, Egypt, and North Africa.

During the Umayyad era, a significant blood feud erupted between the Qahtanites and the northern Adnanite tribes of Qays, known as the Qays-Yaman rivalry, which continued in various forms and degrees until the 19th century. This rivalry fundamentally shaped Arabian tribal politics for over a millennium. The traditional distinction between northern Adnanites and southern Qahtanites became a major organizing principle in Arab society, with the Qahtanites often identified with the 'Yaman' faction in this enduring political divide. Modern historiography suggests this genealogical system may have been systematized during the Umayyad period to organize tribal affiliations and political loyalties.

data.history:
Saba' (Sheba) was an ancient South Arabian kingdom that existed in Yemen from approximately 1000-800 BCE until 275 CE, representing one of the foundational civilizations of the Arabian Peninsula. According to Arab genealogical tradition, Saba is considered the progenitor of the Sabaean people and descended from Qahtan through Yashjub ibn Yarub, making it a foundational Qahtani lineage. The kingdom's heartland was located around Marib and Sirwah, with Marib serving as its primary capital and featuring the famous Marib Dam mentioned in the Quran. The Sabaeans spoke Sabaic, a variety of Old South Arabian, and left behind extensive epigraphic records including over 6,500 Sabaic inscriptions out of 12,000 total Ancient South Arabian inscriptions.

The first major phase of Sabaean civilization lasted from the 8th-1st centuries BCE, reaching its height under rulers called 'mukarrib' (federator). The kingdom achieved its greatest territorial extent under Karib'il Watar, who through protracted warfare extended Sabaean territory from Najran in the north to the Gulf of Aden in the southwest, and eastward to the western foothills of the Hadhramaut plateau. This territorial range would not be seen again until the Himyarite Kingdom over 1,100 years later. The Sabaeans controlled crucial trade routes for frankincense and myrrh, accumulating enormous wealth through the spice trade with Africa, Arabia, and the Mediterranean, with trading partners extending from Khindanu and the Middle Euphrates to Gaza and later Petra.

After declining due to Roman expansion disrupting trade routes, Saba was first annexed by Himyar around the 1st century BCE, but re-emerged from the 1st-3rd centuries CE with a secondary capital established at Sanaa. During this second period, power dynamics had shifted from oasis cities like Marib to highland tribes, and the remarkable Ghumdan Palace was built at Sanaa. The Sabaeans also established a significant presence in the Horn of Africa, founding the kingdom of D'mt in Ethiopia around 800 BCE and maintaining colonies along the Somali coast. The kingdom was permanently conquered by the Himyarites around 275 CE, after which Saba lost its royal status and reverted to a normal tribe limited to the citizens of Marib.

The Sabaeans practiced a theocratic monarchy centered around their national god Almaqah, with four other deities also worshipped: Athtar, Haubas, Dhat-Himyam, and Dhat-Badan. Their religious and cultural influence extended far beyond their political borders through trade networks and conquest. Modern Qahtani tribes across the Arabian Peninsula trace their genealogical heritage to this ancient Sabaean legacy, and the kingdom's memory is preserved in both biblical and Quranic accounts of the Queen of Sheba's visit to King Solomon, though historians consider these accounts legendary. The collapse of traditional South Arabian civilization occurred in the 6th century CE due to multiple factors including political upheaval, the Plague of Justinian, severe droughts, and the breakdown of the Marib Dam system.

folkLegends.title: Queen of Sheba and Solomon
folkLegends.story: The biblical and Quranic accounts describe the legendary Queen of Sheba (known as Bilqis in Islamic tradition and Makeda in Ethiopian tradition) visiting King Solomon of Israel, engaging in trade involving aromatics and gold. This story reflects the kingdom's legendary wealth and international reputation, though it is considered legendary by historians as early first-millennium BCE epigraphic sources show no evidence of diplomatic missions or women rulers.

folkLegends.title: Moses and Princess Tharbis
folkLegends.story: According to the Jewish historian Josephus, Sheba was the home of Princess Tharbis, a Cushite who is said to have been the wife of Moses before he married Zipporah.

data.nameEtymology: The name Saba' carries great prestige among South Arabians and Abyssinians, as it was widely considered to be the birthplace of South Arabian civilization as a whole. The tribal identity was expressed as 'sons of Almaqah' (their national god), reflecting the theocratic nature of Sabaean society.

migrationPath.description: Sabaean migration and/or colonization to establish the kingdom of D'mt in Ethiopia, with the capital at Yeha where a great temple was built for Almaqah

data.history:
The Sudan (Arabic: السودان), singular Al Suwaidi (Arabic: السويدي), is an Arab tribe established across the United Arab Emirates, Qatar and other Gulf states, traditionally serving as close associates of the Bani Yas of Abu Dhabi. By the turn of the 20th century, the settled Sudan numbered approximately 5,000 people distributed across the coastal areas of the Trucial States, with 405 houses in Abu Dhabi and Batin, 250 houses in Dubai, 300 in Sharjah, and 12 in Ajman. The tribe also maintained significant island settlements, with 20 houses on Abu Musa and 40 families on Sirri Island. The Sudan subsisted primarily on fishing and pearling and notably did not own date gardens in the inland oases, distinguishing them from many other tribal groups.

The tribe played crucial roles in Gulf political affairs throughout the 19th and early 20th centuries. In 1824, a dissident faction of Sudan in Sharjah built a tower in Deira with encouragement from Tahnun bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan, leading to conflict when Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi expelled them. This dispute caused both Abu Dhabi and Sharjah to fail sending boats to the pearling in 1825, creating grave economic shock. However, in 1829, approximately 400 Sudan permanently settled Deira after leaving Sharjah in 1826 following disputes with Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi. The tribe's political importance was cemented through marriage alliances, as Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan's mother was a Suwaidi, and he married the daughter of Sudan Sheikh Sultan bin Nasir Al Suwaidi.

In the 1890s, the Sudan attempted territorial expansion when they conceived with Zayed the idea of establishing a fort at Al Zorah in Ajman. In 1897, a section of the Sudan under Sultan bin Nasser requested permission to settle Al Zorah with Zayed's support, which was initially granted by the British Resident. However, the Ruler of Ajman built a defensive fort at one of the waterways, and Sheikh Saqr bin Khalid Al Qasimi appealed to prevent this non-Al Qasimi stronghold. British intervention ultimately blocked the scheme, with Major Percy Cox's visit confirming the decision to abandon the settlement project.

The Sudan also established presence in Qatar, where members from Abu Dhabi settled Bida'a (modern Doha) on Qatar's eastern coast in 1766. However, their Qatar presence faced challenges when British forces suspected the tribe of harboring pirates. In 1801, British representative David Seton attempted to bombard Bida'a but was prevented by shallow waters. More seriously, in February 1841, a British squadron arrived to punish headman Salimayn bin Nasir al-Suwaidi for allegedly harboring the pirate Jasim bin Jabir (Raqraqi), resulting in fines and the destruction of the pirate's ship. The Sudan's control of Bida'a ended in May 1843 when Isa bin Tarif of the Al Bin Ali tribe forced the near-bankrupt Sudan to leave for Lingah. In the modern era, the Al Suwaidi family transitioned into government service and business, with members serving in various governmental and diplomatic roles while maintaining significant influence in contemporary UAE politics and commerce.

data.history: No reliable research data is available for the Thamalah tribe to provide a comprehensive historical narrative. While existing data suggests they may be a Qahtani tribal confederation from southwestern Arabian Peninsula, this cannot be verified without proper source material.

The Yaarubah (Ya'aruba) trace their genealogical origins to ancient Qahtani lineage through Yarub bin Qahtan, positioning them among the earliest Arab settlers in Oman. According to historical sources, during the 8th century BCE, Yaarub, the descendant of Qahtan, is believed to have ruled the entire region of Yemen, including Oman. The Yaarubah are considered to have been the first settlers in Oman from Yemen, arriving even before the major Azd tribal migration that occurred around 120-200 CE following the collapse of the Marib Dam. This early settlement established their presence in the interior regions of Oman, where they became part of the broader Hinawi tribal confederation aligned with the Azd.

The tribe's most significant historical period began in the 17th century when they established the Yaruba dynasty that would rule Oman from 1624 to 1744. The dynasty emerged during a period of Portuguese colonial occupation, with Nasir bin Murshid becoming the first Yaarubah Imam in 1624 when he was elected in Rustaq, marking the beginning of Omani reunification under their leadership. The Yaarubah Imams, operating under the Ibadhi Imamate system, successfully expelled Portuguese colonial forces from Oman in the 1650s and established a powerful maritime empire that extended across the Indian Ocean to East Africa.

Under the Yaruba dynasty, Oman became a major regional power controlling important trade routes in the Indian Ocean. Imam Saif bin Sultan captured Fort Jesus at Mombasa in 1698 after a two-year siege and occupied Bahrain in 1700, demonstrating the extent of Yaarubah maritime influence. The dynasty established colonies along the East African coast including Zanzibar, making Oman a significant competitor to Portuguese and later British imperial interests in the Indian Ocean. Their rule represented the golden age of Omani naval power and territorial expansion.

However, internal rivalries within the house of Yaruba after the death of Imam Sultan in 1718 severely weakened the dynasty. The situation deteriorated when Imam Saif bin Sultan II sought help from Nader Shah of Persia against his rivals in 1737. Persian forces initially aided the Yaruba but eventually rebelled against them in 1743, leading to Persian control of the Omani coast until 1747. After the Omanis expelled the Persians, Ahmed bin Sa'id Albusaidi became the elected Imam in 1744, ending Yaarubah rule and establishing the Al Bu Said dynasty that continues to govern Oman today. The fall of the Yaruba dynasty marked the end of over a century of Yaarubah political dominance in Oman and the broader Gulf region.

data.history:
Banu Kinanah was one of the major Adnani tribes of pre-Islamic Arabia, tracing their lineage to Kinanah ibn Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah. According to traditional Arab genealogy, they descended from Ishmael through Adnan, making them part of the northern Arabian tribal confederation. The tribe was divided into six principal branches: Nadr, Malik, Milkan, Amir, Amr, and Abd Manat. Most significantly, the Nadr branch became the parent tribe of Quraysh, from whom the Prophet Muhammad descended, elevating Kinanah's genealogical importance in Islamic history. Their traditional territory extended from the Tihama coastline near Mecca northeastward to the borders of their tribal relatives, the Banu Asad.

The Abd Manat branch included strong subgroups like Bakr ibn Abd Manat (with Mudlij, Du'il, Layth, and Damra divisions) and formed part of the Ahabish confederation. As polytheists, they worshipped the goddess al-Uzza before Islam. Fihr ibn Malik ibn Nadr emerged as a Kinani leader after their victory against Himyarite forces, and his descendant Qusayy ibn Kilab captured Mecca with Kinani backing, particularly from Ya'mar ibn Amr of the Layth group, though the Bakr opposed Qusayy. The Fijar War erupted when the Kinani exile al-Barrad ibn Qays killed a Banu Kilab chief, leading to conflict between Kilab-Hawazin forces and the Quraysh-Kinanah alliance.

During the early Islamic period, various Kinani groups played different roles in Muhammad's conquests. The Mudlij promised not to attack Quraysh at Badr, while the Ghifar, Layth, and Damra supported Muhammad's conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. The Bakr's attack on Muhammad's allies, the Khuza'a, actually precipitated this conquest. Following the Islamic conquests, Kinanah became a major component of the Arab garrison in Jund Filastin (Palestine), alongside Judham, Lakhm, Khuza'a, Khath'am, and Azd tribes. Caliph Umar appointed two Kinani governors - Alqama ibn Mujazziz in Jerusalem and Alqama ibn Hakim in Lydda around 638-639 CE.

When Crusaders captured Ascalon in 1153, Kinani emirs and fief-holders migrated to Fatimid Egypt, where they were resettled in Damietta by vizier Ṭalāʾīʿ ibn Ruzzīk and became known as the Kinaniyya. Under the Ayyubids, they served as second-tier troops but fled during Louis IX's landing at Damietta in 1249, leading to executions of their commanders by Sultan as-Salih Ayyub. A weakened Kinani presence was still recorded around Mecca in 844/845 CE. Modern descendants of Banu Kinanah are found across Arabia, Iraq, Egypt, Sudan, Palestine, Tunisia, Morocco, Syria, and Yemen, maintaining their status as one of the 'Great Skulls of Arabia' - the historically most powerful pre-Islamic tribes.

folkLegends.title: Origin from Ishmael
folkLegends.story: According to Islamic tradition, the Kinana and other descendants of Ishmael gradually dispersed throughout northern Arabia after Ishmael married a woman of the Yemenite Jurhum tribe and settled in the vicinity of Mecca, but they lost their original faith and fell into idolatry over time.

data.history:
Banu Zayd represents a Najdi tribal lineage that traces its roots to Zayd ibn Ali, who settled in the town of Shaqra in the Najd region of central Arabia. This represents one specific branch among several tribal groups across the Arabian Peninsula that claim descent from ancestors named Zayd, which was a common name in Arabian genealogy. The research does not provide details about the broader genealogical classification (Adnani vs Qahtani) or sub-tribal divisions of this particular Banu Zayd branch.

Unlike many tribal appellations that have become dormant as distinct political entities, this Najdi Banu Zayd branch has maintained significant prominence in modern Saudi Arabia through its members' contributions to religious scholarship, banking, and Islamic institutions. The tribe has produced several notable figures including Muhammad bin Abdul Karim Al-Issa, who serves as Secretary General of the Muslim World League, and members of the Al-Rajhi banking family such as Saleh Abdul Aziz Al Rajhi, co-founder of Al-Rajhi Bank.

The tribe has also contributed significantly to Islamic scholarship and religious leadership. Prominent religious figures from Banu Zayd include Bakr Abu Zayd (1944-2008), a leading Salafi scholar and member of both the Saudi Council of Senior Scholars and the Permanent Committee for Islamic Research and Issuing Fatwas, who served as President of the International Islamic Fiqh Academy from 1985 to 2008. Other notable religious scholars include Abdullah ibn Jibreen, a Saudi Islamic jurist, and Saud Al-Shuraim, who serves as one of the imams and preachers of al-Masjid al-Haram in Mecca.

The research indicates that this particular Banu Zayd branch has successfully adapted to the modern Saudi state structure while maintaining its tribal identity, with members achieving prominence in both religious institutions and the business sector, particularly in Islamic banking through the Al-Rajhi family's establishment of one of the world's largest Islamic banks.
